> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the polarisation curve:** Real fuel cell voltage falls from the Nernst intercept **monotonically** with current density. The **knee** at high j (mass-transport limited) sets the rated power point, typically near **0.6 – 0.7 V per cell** for PEMFC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A answer should be **3 – 5 lines**, with **one equation or diagram** and **one memory anchor** (e.g., "AFC → Apollo").

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**List any three major differences between PEMFC and SOFC with respect to electrolyte, operating temperature, and charge carrier.**

**Model Answer:**
PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell) uses a **polymer electrolyte membrane** (typically Nafion) that conducts **H⁺ ions**, operating at **60 – 90 °C**. In contrast, SOFC (Solid Oxide Fuel Cell) uses a **solid ceramic electrolyte** (yttria-stabilised zirconia, YSZ) that conducts **O²⁻ ions**, operating at **600 – 1000 °C**. Because of the high operating temperature, SOFC can use cheaper **nickel-based catalysts** and can internally reform hydrocarbons, whereas PEMFC requires **platinum catalysts** and high-purity H₂. *(3 marks: 1 mark each difference + 1 for the application contrast.)*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Why is AFC (Alkaline Fuel Cell) unsuitable for operation with unscrubbed atmospheric air?**

**Model Answer:**
The alkaline electrolyte (aqueous KOH) reacts with **CO₂ present in atmospheric air (~420 ppm)** to form potassium carbonate:

$$\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This **carbonate precipitation** blocks the porous electrodes and reduces OH⁻ conductivity, causing rapid performance degradation. Hence AFCs require either **pure O₂** or **CO₂-scrubbed air** (e.g., lime beds). *(3 marks: 1 for reaction, 1 for mechanism, 1 for consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### ❖ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) Compare the working principle, electrolyte, charge carrier, operating temperature, and typical applications of PEMFC and SOFC.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Working Principle:**
Both PEMFC and SOFC are governed by the **same overall cell reaction** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$, but the **half-reactions and ion transport direction differ**.

**PEMFC — acidic, H⁺ carrier, water at cathode:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode:} \quad & \text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \\
\text{Cathode:} \quad & \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

**SOFC — ceramic, O²⁻ carrier, water at anode:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cathode:} \quad & \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{O}^{2-} \\
\text{Anode:} \quad & \text{H}_2 + \text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{e}^-
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Electrolyte:** *(1 mark)*
- PEMFC → **Nafion™ perfluorosulfonic acid polymer membrane**, hydrated, ~50 – 200 µm thick.
- SOFC → **Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia (YSZ) ceramic**, ~10 – 100 µm.

**(iii) Charge Carrier:** *(1 mark)*
- PEMFC → **H⁺** (proton) hopping through sulfonic acid groups.
- SOFC → **O²⁻** hopping through oxygen vacancies in the YSZ lattice.

**(iv) Operating Temperature:** *(1 mark)*
- PEMFC → **60 – 90 °C**, limited by membrane dehydration above ~95 °C.
- SOFC → **600 – 1000 °C**, needed to achieve O²⁻ conductivity > 0.01 S/cm.

**(v) Typical Applications:** *(1 mark)*
- PEMFC → **Light-duty automotive** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO), **backup power**, UAVs.
- SOFC → **Stationary distributed generation** (Bloom Energy 200 kW units), **APU for trucks**, large industrial CHP.

**(vi) Comparative Summary Statement:** *(1 mark)*
*PEMFC trades high cost of Pt catalyst and pure-H₂ requirement for fast start-up and high power density, while SOFC trades slow start-up and brittle ceramic for cheap Ni catalysts, fuel flexibility, and high-grade heat cogeneration.*

---

**(b) Derive the Nernst equation for a H₂/O₂ fuel cell and explain how the operating voltage varies with temperature and pressure.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** For the half-cell reaction $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^-$, the Nernst potential at the anode is:
$$E_a = E_a^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,[\text{H}^+]^2}{1}\right)$$

For the cathode half-reaction $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$:
$$E_c = E_c^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{\text{O}_2}^{0.5}\,[\text{H}^+]^2\right)$$

**Step 2.** The cell voltage $E = E_c - E_a$:
$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{0.5}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$$

This is the **Nernst equation** for an H₂/O₂ fuel cell. *(2 marks derived)*

**Step 3. Effect of Temperature.** Using $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ and $\left(\partial E/\partial T\right)_P = \Delta S/nF$:
- For the H₂/O₂ cell, $\Delta S < 0$ (entropy decreases as 1.5 mol gas → 1 mol liquid), so **E decreases with rising T**.
- At 25 °C: $E = 1.229$ V (liquid water product).
- At 200 °C (steam product): $E = 1.18$ V.
- At 800 °C (SOFC, steam): $E \approx 0.95$ V. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4. Effect of Pressure.** From the Nernst expression, increasing $P_{\text{H}_2}$ or $P_{\text{O}_2}$ *raises* $E$ logarithmically. Raising total pressure from 1 atm to 10 atm increases $E$ by:

$$\Delta E = \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(10) = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{2 \times 96{,}485} \times 2.303 = 0.030\ \text{V}$$

A modest **30 mV gain** is achievable by pressurisation. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5. Conclusion.**
The Nernst equation shows that **operating voltage is sensitive primarily to temperature (decreases) and logarithmically to reactant partial pressures (increases)**. Real cell voltage is further reduced by **activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials**, dropping the working point to 0.6 – 0.8 V. *(1 mark)*

---

### ❖ Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC). Mention its electrolyte, electrodes, and typical operating conditions. State any two advantages and two disadvantages.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Construction:** *(2 marks)*
A PAFC consists of:
- **Anode** — porous graphite with Pt catalyst (~0.3 mg/cm²).
- **Cathode** — porous graphite with Pt catalyst (~0.5 mg/cm²).
- **Electrolyte** — concentrated **H₃PO₄ (~100 %)** immobilised in a **silicon carbide (SiC) matrix** that retains the acid by capillary action.
- **Bipolar plates** — graphite composite with flow channels for gas distribution.

**(ii) Electrode Reactions:** *(2 marks)*
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode:} \quad & \text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \\
\text{Cathode:} \quad & \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Net:} \quad & \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

Water is produced at the cathode and removed by the flowing air stream.

**(iii) Operating Conditions:** *(1 mark)*
- Temperature: **160 – 220 °C** (above the freezing point of H₃PO₄ at 42 °C and below its dehydration regime).
- Pressure: 1 – 8 atm.
- Voltage per cell: ~0.65 V at 250 mA/cm².

**(iv) Advantages:** *(1 mark)*
1. **CO tolerance up to ~1 %** (vs ~10 ppm for PEMFC) — tolerant of reformed hydrocarbon fuels.
2. **High-grade waste heat (~200 °C)** usable for cogeneration — overall CHP efficiency 70 – 80 %.

**(v) Disadvantages:** *(1 mark)*
1. **Corrosive phosphoric acid** — requires expensive corrosion-resistant stack materials.
2. **Lower electrical efficiency (~40 %)** compared with PEMFC or SOFC.

---

**(b) Discuss the relative merits and demerits of Low-Temperature Fuel Cells (PEMFC, AFC) versus High-Temperature Fuel Cells (MCFC, SOFC). In which applications is each group preferred?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Low-Temperature Fuel Cells (AFC, PEMFC, DMFC) — Merits:** *(2 marks)*
1. **Fast start-up** — seconds to minutes; ideal for automotive and portable uses.
2. **Better durability under thermal cycling** — no ceramic brittleness, no molten electrolyte freezing.
3. **High power density** — PEMFC reaches ~1 W/cm² due to thin electrolyte and fast kinetics with Pt.
4. **Sealed, compact packaging** — easier to integrate into vehicles and electronics.

**(ii) Low-Temperature Fuel Cells — Demerits:** *(1 mark)*
1. Require **expensive Pt catalysts**.
2. **CO intolerance** — ≤10 ppm in PEMFC, hence external reformer + PROX required.
3. Low-grade heat (≤90 °C) of limited CHP value.

**(iii) High-Temperature Fuel Cells (MCFC, SOFC) — Merits:** *(2 marks)*
1. **Cheap Ni-based catalysts** — no noble metal needed.
2. **Fuel flexibility** — internal reforming of CH₄, CO, even diesel possible.
3. **High-grade heat (650 – 850 °C)** usable for bottoming steam/gas turbine → **85 – 90 % total CHP efficiency**.
4. **No water management issues** — water produced as steam, easily removed.

**(iv) High-Temperature Fuel Cells — Demerits:** *(1 mark)*
1. **Slow start-up** (hours) — thermal cycling induces ceramic cracking and electrolyte freezing.
2. **Sealing and corrosion problems** — molten carbonate corrosion; SOFC brittle ceramic.
3. **Expensive stack materials** — high-T stainless steels, ceramic interconnects.

**(v) Preferred Applications:** *(1 mark)*
- **Low-T FCs (PEMFC, AFC):** Passenger cars (Toyota Mirai), buses, forklifts, submarines (Type 212, Germany), spacecraft (AFC, Apollo).
- **High-T FCs (MCFC, SOFC):** Multi-MW stationary power plants (FuelCell Energy DFC3000), industrial CHP, microgrid distributed generation (Bloom Energy), truck APU.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Fuel Cell Comparison Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong charge-carrier / electrolyte pairing** — *3-mark loss per occurrence.* SOFC carries **O²⁻**, NOT H⁺. PEMFC carries **H⁺**, NOT OH⁻. MCFC carries **CO₃²⁻**, NOT O²⁻.
>
> 2. **Confusing PEMFC with PAFC** — both conduct H⁺, but PAFC uses **liquid H₃PO₄** (not a polymer membrane). PEMFC temperature ≤ 95 °C; PAFC ≈ 200 °C.
>
> 3. **Quoting 100 % efficiency** — many students naively claim "fuel cell efficiency = 100 %". The correct thermodynamic ceiling is $\Delta G/\Delta H = 0.83$; the practical ceiling for electrical efficiency is **40 – 60 %**. Use $\eta = E_{\text{cell}}/E_{\text{th}} \times 100$.
>
> 4. **Wrong sign in Nernst equation** — the formula is $E = E^{\circ} + (RT/nF)\ln Q$ (with **+ sign** for the **reactant-favoured** form). Some textbooks write it with $-\ln Q$ — pick one and **stick to it**.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the difference between electrical efficiency and CHP efficiency** — SOFC electrical η ~ 50 % but **CHP η ~ 85 %**. Examiners expect the distinction.
>
> 6. **Mixing up AFC applications** — AFC is *not* for automotive use; it is **space/defence** (Apollo, Shuttle). PEMFC dominates transport.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert units** — temperature in K in Nernst equation, NOT °C. Always write $T = 298 + t(^\circ\text{C})$.
>
> 8. **Writing the catalyst as "Pt only"** — for SOFC and MCFC the catalyst is **Ni-based**, not platinum. Examiners dock 2 marks for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fuel Cell Comparison**
>
> - **Six families:** AFC, PEMFC, DMFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC.
> - **Charge carrier mnemonic:** **"A-P-D-P-M-S" → "OH⁻ – H⁺ – H⁺ – H⁺ – CO₃²⁻ – O²⁻"** *(AF-PEM-DM-PAF-MC-SO)*.
> - **Operating temperature bins:** Low (AFC, PEMFC, DMFC) < 100 °C, Mid (PAFC) ~200 °C, High (MCFC ~650 °C, SOFC ~800 °C).
> - **PEMFC mantra:** "Pure H₂, Pt catalyst, ≤90 °C, automotive king."
> - **SOFC mantra:** "YSZ, O²⁻, 800 °C, Ni catalyst, fuel-flexible, slow start."
> - **AFC mantra:** "Apollo fuel cell — alkaline, OH⁻, CO₂ must be scrubbed."
> - **PAFC mantra:** "First commercial stationary CHP — H₃PO₄, 200 °C, CO-tolerant."
> - **MCFC mantra:** "Molten carbonate, CO₃²⁻, CO₂ recycled at cathode, multi-MW."
> - **DMFC mantra:** "Liquid methanol, no reformer, low η, portable."
> - **Efficiency rank (electrical, LHV):** SOFC ≈ MCFC ≈ AFC (45 – 60 %) > PEMFC (40 – 60 %) > PAFC (35 – 45 %) > DMFC (20 – 30 %).
> - **CHP efficiency rank:** SOFC ~ 90 % > MCFC ~ 85 % > PAFC ~ 80 % > AFC ~ 80 % > PEMFC ~ 70 %.
> - **Theoretical cell voltage:** $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V (25 °C, liquid H₂O), 1.481 V if using ΔH.
> - **Nernst equation:** $E = E^{\circ} + (RT/2F)\ln\left(P_{\text{H}_2} P_{\text{O}_2}^{0.5} / P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}\right)$.
> - **Operating cell voltage under load:** 0.6 – 0.8 V per cell.
> - **Polarisation losses:** Activation (Tafel) → low j; Ohmic (linear) → mid j; Concentration → high j.
> - **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; **n = 2** for H₂/O₂.
> - **Thermodynamic ceiling efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{th}} = \Delta G / \Delta H = 0.83$ at 25 °C.
> - **Apollo fuel cell** = AFC; **Toyota Mirai** = PEMFC; **Bloom Energy Server** = SOFC; **FuelCell Energy DFC3000** = MCFC; **PC-25 (ONSI)** = PAFC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Combustion Engines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Combustion Engines

# 🔥 Hydrogen Combustion Engines — KTU 2024 Premium Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Official Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> A **Hydrogen Combustion Engine** is a thermodynamic energy conversion device that releases the chemical energy bound in molecular hydrogen ($H_2$) by reacting it with an oxidizer (typically air) inside a controlled combustion chamber, converting the released thermal energy into mechanical work via a piston-crank assembly (reciprocating) or rotary mechanism.

### 1.1 Why Hydrogen in an Engine? — The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a regular petrol engine as a person eating a heavy **starchy meal** (long hydrocarbon chains, like $C_8H_{18}$). The body takes time, enzymes, and many steps to break it down, releasing energy slowly and producing lots of "waste" (CO, CO₂, soot, unburnt hydrocarbons).

Now picture **hydrogen** as a simple, **pure glucose shot** — already small, instantly digestible, and leaving behind only one harmless byproduct: **water vapor**. Hydrogen reacts with oxygen in a single, violent, clean step:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Energy}$$

So a hydrogen combustion engine is essentially the *same mechanical skeleton* as a petrol/diesel engine, but fed with a fuel whose molecular simplicity translates into cleaner combustion, higher flame speeds, and wider flammability limits.

### 1.2 Classification of Hydrogen Combustion Engines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Classification (Must-Memorize):**
>
> | Engine Type | Ignition Mode | Hydrogen Supply |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Hydrogen SI Engine** | Spark Ignition (external) | Carburetor / Port Injection / Direct Injection |
> | **Hydrogen CI Engine** | Compression Ignition (auto) | Difficult — high auto-ignition temp (~570 °C) |
> | **Hydrogen HCCI Engine** | Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition | Dual fuel mode |
> | **Hydrogen Gas Turbine** | Continuous combustion | Aviation & power generation |

### 1.3 Why Hydrogen is Different from Petrol/Diesel

Hydrogen possesses several **physical-chemical oddities** that simultaneously make it an *excellent* and a *troublesome* engine fuel:

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen's "Engine-Relevant" Physical Constants (KTU Board Facts):**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV): 119.96 MJ/kg** (≈ 2.7× higher than gasoline per kg)
> - **Flammability Limits:** 4–75 % by volume in air (extraordinarily wide vs gasoline 1–7.6 %)
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature:** **571 °C** (vs gasoline ~250 °C)
> - **Minimum Ignition Energy:** **0.02 mJ** (≈ 1/10 of gasoline)
> - **Laminar Flame Speed:** **2.5–3.5 m/s** (≈ 6× faster than gasoline)
> - **Diffusion Coefficient:** Highest of all gases (leak-prone)
> - **Octane Number:** **>130** (resists knock exceptionally)

These figures govern *every* design choice a hydrogen engine demands.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Hydrogen Combustion in an Engine

The complete thermodynamic cycle for a **hydrogen SI engine** operates on the classical **Otto Cycle** (constant volume heat addition), with hydrogen playing the role of the working fuel.

**Step-by-step logical flow inside the cylinder:**

1. **Intake Stroke (Suction):** Hydrogen (either premixed with air in the intake manifold or directly injected) enters the cylinder. Because of hydrogen's low density, the mass of fuel inducted per cycle is *very low*, demanding **larger displacement volumes** for equivalent power.
2. **Compression Stroke:** The $H_2$-air mixture is compressed. Due to hydrogen's high octane number (>130), **very high compression ratios (CR up to 13:1)** are physically possible without knock — unlike petrol which knocks beyond ~10:1.
3. **Combustion (Power Stroke):** Spark plug ignites the mixture. The flame propagates at ~3 m/s (very fast), causing near-instantaneous pressure rise. The combustion equation is stoichiometrically:
$$2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76 N_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76 N_2$$
4. **Exhaust Stroke:** Only $H_2O$ (water vapor), $N_2$, and small quantities of $NO_x$ (due to high flame temperatures) are expelled. **Zero CO₂ emissions** — the KTU-favoured highlight.

### 2.2 Hydrogen CI Engines — Why They Are Rare

Compression ignition requires the fuel to **self-ignite** at the temperature reached after compression. Petrol auto-ignites at ~250 °C, but **hydrogen needs 571 °C** — beyond the practical adiabatic flame temperature of compressed air (≈ 450 °C at CR = 18:1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A pure hydrogen CI engine is *theoretically* possible only with unrealistically high CR (>22:1). Hence, hydrogen **CI engines are practically realized as HCCI / dual-fuel engines** using a small pilot diesel injection to ignite hydrogen.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield. Memorize the table before any Part-B attempt.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | Stoichiometric reaction | mol |
| 2 | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{34.56}{4} = 8.64$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel mass ratio (by mass) | kg air / kg fuel |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 2.38$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel **molar** ratio | mol air / mol fuel |
| 4 | $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 119.96 \;\text{MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value of hydrogen | MJ/kg |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{otto}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Air-standard Otto efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Compression ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{W} = \eta_{th} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}$ | Brake power from fuel flow | kW |
| 8 | $NO_x \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{flame}}\right)$ | Zeldovich-type $NO_x$ formation | ppm |
| 9 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} = h A (T_{flame} - T_{wall})$ | Heat loss to cylinder walls | W |
| 10 | $P_{me} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_s \cdot N / K}$ | Mean effective pressure | Pa |

Where:
- $r$ = compression ratio
- $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio (≈ 1.4 for air)
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel
- $V_s$ = swept volume
- $N$ = RPM, $K$ = number of power strokes per cycle

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU stresses this topic (board reasoning):**
> - Hydrogen SI engines are the **retrofit pathway** for existing automotive ICE fleets — no new fuel cell investment required.
> - Aviation (Pratt & Whitney, Airbus ZEROe demonstrator) and heavy transport are testing hydrogen **gas turbine combustion**.
> - Hydrogen combustion is the only practical non-electrified pathway for long-haul trucks and ships in a carbon-neutral future.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass Basis)

**Given:** Combustion reaction of hydrogen in air.

**Step 1 — Write balanced reaction with nitrogen from air (air = $O_2 + 3.76 N_2$):**

$$2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76 N_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76 N_2$$

**Step 2 — Identify molar masses involved:**

- $H_2$ molecular weight = $2 \times 1.008 = 2.016 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $O_2$ molecular weight = $32 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $N_2$ molecular weight = $28 \;\text{g/mol}$
- Air molecular weight (with $3.76 N_2$): $M_{\text{air}} = 32 + (3.76)(28) = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 \;\text{g/mol}$

**Step 3 — Mass of fuel per stoichiometric reaction:**

$$m_f = 2 \times M_{H_2} = 2 \times 2.016 = 4.032 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air supplied per stoichiometric reaction:**

$$m_{\text{air}} = 1 \times M_{O_2} + 3.76 \times M_{N_2} = 32 + (3.76)(28) = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{\text{stoich, mass}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_f} = \dfrac{137.28}{4.032} \\
&= 34.05 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The widely-quoted textbook value of **34.56** uses $M_{air} = 28.97$ (standard air). Either answer (~34) is acceptable, but **state the molecular weight of air you assumed** to score full marks.

**Step 6 — Volume ratio (informative):** Since both gases behave ideally at STP:

$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich, vol}} = \dfrac{(1 + 3.76) \times 22.4}{2 \times 22.4} = \dfrac{4.76}{2} = 2.38 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ air} / \text{m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Released per kg of Hydrogen

**Given:** $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96 \;\text{MJ/kg}$ and per kmol the enthalpy of combustion = $241.83 \;\text{MJ/kmol}$.

**Step 1 — Convert per-kmol to per-kg:**

$$LHV = \dfrac{241.83 \;\text{MJ/kmol}}{2.016 \;\text{kg/kmol}} = 119.96 \;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compare volumetric energy density at STP:**

- Hydrogen at STP: $\rho = 0.0899 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Energy per cubic meter: $E_v = 119.96 \times 0.0899 = 10.79 \;\text{MJ/m}^3$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Punchline:** Hydrogen's *mass* energy density is excellent, but its *volumetric* energy density is **~3000× lower than petrol** — this drives all hydrogen storage research (compressed gas, liquid, metal hydrides).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Efficiency of Hydrogen SI Engine (Otto Cycle)

**Given:** Compression ratio $r$, specific heat ratio $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Step 1 — Otto cycle processes:**

- 1 → 2: Isentropic compression
- 2 → 3: Constant-volume heat addition (combustion)
- 3 → 4: Isentropic expansion (power)
- 4 → 1: Constant-volume heat rejection

**Step 2 — Apply 1st law to closed cycle:**

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 3 — For ideal gas with constant $c_v$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} &= \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use isentropic relations:**

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma - 1}, \qquad \dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{otto}} &= 1 - \dfrac{T_1 (T_4/T_1 - 1)}{T_2 (T_3/T_2 - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an ideal cycle (where $T_4/T_1 = T_3/T_2$), this reduces to the famous result:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{otto}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU-style):** For $r = 12$, $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{otto}} &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{12^{0.4}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3.105} = 1 - 0.322 \\
&= 0.678 \;\;(67.8\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare this with a petrol engine at $r = 8$: $\eta = 1 - 1/8^{0.4} = 1 - 1/2.297 = 56.5\%$. **Hydrogen's higher knock-resistance allows 11% higher efficiency gain purely from CR increase.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison: Hydrogen vs Petrol vs Diesel

| Parameter | Hydrogen SI | Petrol SI | Diesel CI |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Compression Ratio | 12–13 | 8–10 | 14–18 |
| Thermal Efficiency (Otto / Diesel) | 38–42 % | 25–30 % | 35–40 % |
| Power Output (% of petrol baseline) | 85–100 % | 100 % | 100 % |
| $CO_2$ Emission | **0 g/km** | 120–180 g/km | 100–160 g/km |
| $NO_x$ Emission | High (without EGR) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Knocking Tendency | Very low | Moderate | N/A |
| Cold Start | Hard (high $T_{ign}$) | Easy | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen SI Engine — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2TANK[Hydrogen Storage Tank]:::blk
    PRV[Pressure Regulator]:::blk
    INJ[Hydrogen Injector]:::blk
    AIR[Air Intake Manifold]:::blk
    MIX[Intake Manifold Mixing]:::blk

    SPK[Spark Plug]:::blk
    EXH[Exhaust Manifold H2O plus N2]:::blk
    CRANK[Crankshaft Mechanical Output]:::out

    H2TANK --> PRV --> INJ --> MIX
    AIR --> MIX
    MIX --> CYL
    SPK --> CYL
    CYL --> EXH
    CYL --> CRANK

    classDef blk fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

```mermaid
    end

```

### 4.3 Hydrogen Engine Combustion Process — Cycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intake Stroke H2 plus Air Inducted] --> B[Compression Stroke High CR]
    B --> C[Combustion Power Stroke Fast Flame]
    C --> D[Exhaust Stroke H2O plus N2 plus NOx]
    D --> A

    C -.-> C1[Spark Initiated]
    C -.-> C2[Laminar Flame Speed 3 m per s]
    C -.-> C3[Pressure Rise nearly Instantaneous]
```

### 4.4 Abnormal Combustion — Causes & Mitigations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ABN[Abnormal Combustion in H2 Engine]:::root --> BF[Backfire]
    ABN --> PI[Pre Ignition]
    ABN --> KN[Knocking Rare in H2]

    BF --> BF1[Cause: Residual H2 in Intake]
    BF --> BF2[Cause: Hot Spots on Intake Valve]
    BF --> BF3[Mitigation: Backfire Arrestor]
    BF --> BF4[Mitigation: Water Injection]

    PI --> PI1[Cause: Auto Ignition at 571 C]
    PI --> PI2[Mitigation: Cooler Charge Air]

    KN --> KN1[Hydrogen Octane >130 So Rare]
    KN --> KN2[Mitigation: Not Required]

    classDef root fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**List any three unique properties of hydrogen that affect its combustion behaviour in an engine. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Very wide flammability limits (4–75 vol% in air):** Allows operation on very lean mixtures, improving efficiency and lowering flame temperature (reducing $NO_x$).
2. **High auto-ignition temperature (~571 °C):** Prevents compression ignition, making hydrogen CI engines impractical without pilot fuel.
3. **Very low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ):** Causes frequent backfire due to residual hot gases igniting the fresh charge; demands backfire arrestors.
*(Any 3 valid properties: high flame speed, high octane >130, low density, high diffusivity etc.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Why are hydrogen SI engines preferred over hydrogen CI engines? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen has a **very high auto-ignition temperature (571 °C)** compared to diesel (~250 °C). In a CI engine, only adiabatic compression heats the air; even at CR of 22:1, the temperature reached is insufficient to auto-ignite hydrogen. Hence pure hydrogen CI engines are **impractical**, while hydrogen SI engines exploit hydrogen's **high octane number (>130)** to permit **higher compression ratios** (up to 13:1) yielding better thermal efficiency than petrol SI engines.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** — **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the working of a **hydrogen-fueled Spark Ignition (SI) engine**. Discuss the role of fuel injection modes (carburetion, port injection, direct injection) on performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen SI engine operates on the ideal Otto cycle with compression ratio $r = 12$. The conditions at the beginning of compression are $P_1 = 100 \;\text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 320 \;\text{K}$. The heat added is $Q_{\text{in}} = 1500 \;\text{kJ/kg}$. Assuming $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_v = 0.717 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$, calculate:
(i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Maximum temperature after combustion, (iii) Maximum pressure in the cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Hydrogen SI Engine:**
- Hydrogen from a high-pressure tank is regulated to ~1.5–3 bar and either (i) **carbureted / port-injected** upstream of the intake valve, or (ii) **directly injected** into the cylinder at high pressure (200+ bar).
- During the intake stroke, a homogeneous $H_2$ + air mixture enters. In **port injection**, mixture homogeneity is excellent; backfire risk is higher. In **direct injection**, hydrogen is injected late → stratified charge, suppresses backfire, allows higher loads.
- Compression: CR up to 13:1 thanks to high octane rating of $H_2$.
- Combustion: Spark plug fires; flame propagates rapidly (~3 m/s). Power stroke follows.
- Exhaust: Only $H_2O$ vapor and $N_2$ (plus $NO_x$) emitted.
- *Performance:* Port injection → simpler, lower $NO_x$ at part load; Direct injection → higher power, lower backfire, better cold start.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{otto}} &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{12^{0.4}} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{1}{3.105} = 1 - 0.322 = 0.678
\end{aligned}
$$
**$\eta_{\text{otto}} = 67.8\%$**

**(ii) Maximum Temperature ($T_3$) after combustion:**
First, $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 320 \times 3.105 = 993.6 \;\text{K}$

Heat added at constant volume:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{in}} &= c_v (T_3 - T_2) \\
1500 &= 0.717 (T_3 - 993.6) \\
T_3 - 993.6 &= 2092.05 \\
T_3 &= 3085.6 \;\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $T_3$ is limited by dissociation of $H_2O$ above ~2800 K, but for the ideal Otto cycle the textbook value stands.

**(iii) Maximum Pressure ($P_3$):**
First, $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 12^{1.4} = 100 \times 32.42 = 3242 \;\text{kPa}$

Constant-volume heating:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_3}{P_2} &= \dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = \dfrac{3085.6}{993.6} = 3.105 \\
P_3 &= 3242 \times 3.105 = 10066 \;\text{kPa} \approx 10.07 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q4. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** — **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss the major **abnormal combustion problems** encountered in hydrogen engines (backfire, pre-ignition, knocking) and the engineering modifications used to overcome them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio** (by mass and by volume) for hydrogen burning in air, and determine the energy released per kg of fuel. Comment on the engineering significance of the volumetric energy density. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Abnormal Combustion in Hydrogen Engines:**
1. **Backfire** — flame propagation back into the intake manifold. **Causes:** residual $H_2$ in manifold ignited by hot intake valve or backflow of hot exhaust gases; very low ignition energy (0.02 mJ). **Mitigation:** backfire arrestors (flame traps), water injection into intake, late intake valve closing, **direct injection** of hydrogen.
2. **Pre-ignition** — auto-ignition of charge before spark. **Cause:** hot spots (carbon deposits from trace oil, glowing spark plug electrodes). **Mitigation:** cooler intake charge, water/methanol injection, lower CR, hydrogen-compatible lubricants reducing deposits.
3. **Knocking** — extremely rare for $H_2$ due to octane >130, but can occur in HCCI mode at very high loads. **Mitigation:** lean burn operation reduces knocking tendency.

**Engineering Modifications:** Hardened valve seats, brass/copper-bronze exhaust valves, low-sodium-cooled exhaust valves, stainless-steel head, special spark plugs with wider gap (~1.0 mm), hydrogen-compatible oils (zinc-free), reinforced piston rings, exhaust heat recovery for $H_2O$ management.

**(b) Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio & Energy Calculation:**

**By mass:** (Derived in §3.1 above)
$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{137.28}{4.032} \approx 34.0 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**By volume:** (Derived in §3.1 above)
$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich, vol}} = 2.38 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ air/m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

**Energy released per kg:**
$$Q = \text{LHV} = 119.96 \;\text{MJ/kg} \approx 120 \;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Energy per cubic meter at STP:**
$$E_v = 119.96 \times 0.0899 = 10.79 \;\text{MJ/m}^3$$

**Engineering Significance:**
Volumetric energy density of hydrogen at STP is only **10.79 MJ/m³** versus petrol's **~32,000 MJ/m³** — a factor of ~3000× lower. This is why on-board hydrogen storage requires either:
- **Compression at 350–700 bar** (raising $E_v$ to ~4,500–8,500 MJ/m³)
- **Liquefaction at 20 K** ($E_v \approx 8,500 \;\text{MJ/m}^3$)
- **Metal hydrides / LOHCs** (chemically bound storage)

Without these, a hydrogen car would need a fuel tank the size of a small house to match the range of a petrol car.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Don't confuse LHV (119.96 MJ/kg) with HHV (141.86 MJ/kg).** For engine calculations, always use LHV since exhaust $H_2O$ leaves as vapor.
> - **Stoichiometric A/F for $H_2$ is 34:1, not 14.7:1** (the 14.7:1 value is for petrol).
> - **Don't claim hydrogen CI engines are common** — they are largely experimental. KTU examiners mark this strictly.
> - **Always state $\gamma = 1.4$** for Otto cycle derivations unless the question provides a different value.
> - Forgetting to use $\ln$ or $r^{\gamma-1}$ correctly loses 2 marks instantly in Part-B.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4 — Hydrogen Combustion Engines):**
> - **Stoichiometric A/F (mass) = 34:1** for $H_2$ in air; molar ratio = **2.38:1**.
> - **LHV of $H_2$ = 119.96 MJ/kg**; HHV = 141.86 MJ/kg. Always use LHV for engines.
> - **Flammability limits:** 4–75 vol% (extremely wide).
> - **Auto-ignition temperature:** 571 °C → CI engine impractical without pilot fuel.
> - **Octane number:** >130 → high CR possible → high Otto efficiency.
> - **Hydrogen SI engine efficiency** for $r=12$ → $\eta = 67.8\%$ (ideal Otto).
> - **$NO_x$ is the main pollutant** → use lean burn, EGR, water injection to suppress.
> - **Backfire is the dominant abnormal combustion** → fix with direct injection + backfire arrestors.
> - **HCCI / dual-fuel** is the practical solution for hydrogen "CI" engines.
> - **Cold-start difficulty** due to high auto-ignition temperature and low flame stability at low temp.
> - **Volumetric energy density of $H_2$ at STP is ~10.79 MJ/m³** — drives storage technology choice.
> - **Zero $CO_2$** tailpipe emission is the central environmental benefit highlighted by KTU.
> - Know the **Otto cycle formula** $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ and the constant-volume relation $P_3/P_2 = T_3/T_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Turbines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Turbines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Hydrogen Turbine** (also called a **Hydrogen-Fueled Gas Turbine** or **H2-GT**) is a rotating turbomachine that converts the chemical energy liberated from the combustion of hydrogen gas (or hydrogen-rich fuel blends) with an oxidizer (typically compressed air) into mechanical shaft work, which is then coupled to an electrical generator, compressor, or industrial load. According to the KTU 2024 OECHT616 syllabus, hydrogen turbines occupy a critical position in the **hydrogen value chain** as the *end-use power-conversion device* that enables dispatchable, large-scale, zero-carbon electricity generation when coupled with green hydrogen.

The combustion reaction that drives the turbine is:

$$\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$$

The principal energy release values (standard conditions, 298 K, 1 atm) are:

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ = 119.96 MJ/kg** (≈ 242 MJ/kg-NOx-free basis in many references)
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂ = 141.88 MJ/kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike fuel cells (electrochemical, ~50–60% efficient, modular), hydrogen turbines use **direct thermal combustion** to drive a **Brayton cycle**, achieving 35–45% simple-cycle efficiency and **up to 60–62% in combined-cycle (CCGT) configuration**, making them essential for utility-scale baseload power.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a hydrogen turbine as a **"windmill powered by fire instead of wind."**

- A conventional wind turbine spins blades because wind rushes past them.
- A steam turbine spins blades because high-pressure steam (from burning coal/gas in a boiler) pushes them.
- A **hydrogen gas turbine** is similar to a jet engine: air is sucked in, compressed, mixed with hydrogen fuel, **ignited**, and the resulting hot, expanding gases rush past turbine blades, spinning them at thousands of RPM. The only difference is the *fuel* — instead of kerosene or natural gas, we burn pure hydrogen or a hydrogen-natural-gas blend.

The catch? Hydrogen burns **far hotter and faster** than natural gas, so the turbine's metal heart must be redesigned to survive the flames.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why care about hydrogen turbines?**
> - They convert intermittent renewable electricity into **firm, dispatchable power** when paired with hydrogen storage (power-to-gas-to-power).
> - They serve as the **only commercially available, MW-to-GW scale, zero-CO₂ thermal power technology** today.
> - They can be **retrofitted** to existing natural gas infrastructure — a critical bridge technology for India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (target: 5 MMT annual production by 2030).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants Used in Hydrogen Turbine Analysis

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|-------|------|
| LHV of H₂ | $Q_{LHV}$ | **119.96** | MJ/kg |
| HHV of H₂ | $Q_{HHV}$ | **141.88** | MJ/kg |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | $(A/F)_{st}$ | **34.33** | kg air/kg H₂ |
| Adiabatic flame temperature (stoich.) | $T_{ad}$ | **2400–2823** | K |
| Laminar flame speed (in air) | $S_L$ | **2.0–3.5** | m/s (vs ~0.4 for methane) |
| Flammability limits (in air, vol%) | — | **4–75** | % |
| Auto-ignition temperature | — | **858** | K (≈ 585 °C) |
| Minimum ignition energy | — | **0.017** | mJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These unique properties — **high flame speed, broad flammability, low ignition energy, and very high flame temperature** — are precisely what make hydrogen a *powerful* turbine fuel but also what create the design challenges discussed in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Brayton Cycle Foundation

A hydrogen gas turbine operates on the **open Brayton cycle**. The four ideal processes are:

1. **Process 1→2 — Isentropic Compression** (in axial/centrifugal compressor)
2. **Process 2→3 — Constant-Pressure Heat Addition** (in combustor: $\text{H}_2 + \text{Air} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H$)
3. **Process 3→4 — Isentropic Expansion** (in turbine)
4. **Process 4→1 — Constant-Pressure Heat Rejection** (exhaust to atmosphere)

### 2.2 Why Hydrogen Cannot Simply Replace Natural Gas — The Core Engineering Challenges

| # | Challenge | Root Cause (H₂ Property) | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Flashback / Reverse Flame Propagation** | Flame speed $S_L$ is ~6–8× methane | Lean-premixed (LPM) or Micromix combustors; fuel staging |
| 2 | **Auto-ignition inside compressor** | Broad flammability (4–75% vol), low ignition energy (0.017 mJ) | Inert purge with N₂/CO₂ before shutdown; strict temperature control |
| 3 | **Extreme NOx formation** | Adiabatic flame temp $T_{ad}$ > 2400 K | Lean-burn (T₃ < 1850 K); steam dilution; Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) |
| 4 | **Material degradation (Hydrogen Embrittlement)** | H atoms diffuse into metal lattice, causing cracking | Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 347H), nickel-based superalloys, internal coatings |
| 5 | **Water vapor in exhaust (high H₂O%)** | Combustion product is 100% H₂O | Hot-end corrosion; modified blade cooling with steam instead of air |
| 6 | **Reduced mass flow per unit energy** | H₂ has very low density (0.0899 kg/m³ at STP, vs 0.8 for CH₄) | Larger fuel manifolds; cryogenic or compressed storage near turbine |

### 2.3 Classification of Hydrogen Turbine Combustion Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Frequently Tested Topic:** Examiner often asks to *compare* these combustion modes.

- **A. Lean Premixed (LPM) Combustion** — Fuel and air are premixed at high air/fuel ratios before combustion. Lower flame temperature → low NOx. **Risk of flashback.**
- **B. Rich-Quench-Lean (RQL)** — Fuel-rich primary zone (low O₂ suppresses NOx), then rapid air dilution. Robust, low flashback. **Used in modern GE H-class.**
- **C. Catalytic Combustion** — Catalyst surface initiates low-T oxidation (~700–900 K). Ultra-low NOx, but catalyst durability issues.
- **D. Micro-Mix Combustion** — Hundreds of tiny flame jets; high mixing uniformity. **State-of-the-art.**
- **E. Sequential Combustion (SEV)** — Two combustors in series (e.g., Siemens HL-class). Reheats gases for higher efficiency.

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` inside table rows to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning / Where Used |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric combustion | $\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O}$ | Basis for all mass/energy balances |
| 2 | Mass-basis LHV energy released | $Q = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2}$ | Total thermal input to combustor [kW] |
| 3 | Air-Fuel ratio (stoich., mass) | $(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{34.33}{1}$ kg air / kg H₂ | Used for combustion air demand |
| 4 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | $\phi < 1$ ⇒ lean burn (low NOx) |
| 5 | Brayton net work (ideal gas) | $W_{net} = c_p (T_3 - T_4) - c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Turbine work minus compressor work |
| 6 | Thermal efficiency (ideal Brayton) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Function of pressure ratio $r_p$ only |
| 7 | Optimum pressure ratio | $r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma/2(\gamma-1)}$ | Where $\eta_{th}$ is maximized |
| 8 | Turbine inlet temperature (TIT) | $T_3$ | Modern H-class: $T_3 \approx 1600\text{–}1700\ \text{°C}$ |
| 9 | NOx index (Zeldovich thermal) | $[NO_x] \propto \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{2R_u T_{ad}}\right)$ | Exponential sensitivity to peak flame temp |
| 10 | Power output (gross) | $\dot{W}_g = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_4) \cdot \eta_T$ | Shaft output before auxiliaries |
| 11 | Specific power | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{\dot{m}}$ | kJ/kg of working fluid |
| 12 | Carnot upper bound (for reference) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_{cold}/T_{hot}$ | Theoretical ceiling for any heat engine |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen turbines are deployed today in three major roles:

- **Utility-scale firm renewable power:** Solar/wind → electrolyzer → H₂ storage → H₂ turbine during peak demand. Examples: GE's 7F.05 (84 MW) certified for 30–50% H₂ blends; Siemens SGT-A35 (B-class) demonstrated at pure H₂.
- **Industrial combined heat and power (CHP):** Refineries and steel plants use by-product H₂ in dedicated turbines for in-house electricity and process steam.
- **Backup power for data centers and hospitals:** Hydrogen stored in underground caverns feeds turbines during grid outages — a fuel-independent alternative to diesel gensets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Brayton Cycle Efficiency with Hydrogen Combustion

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **guaranteed derivation** the KTU examiner loves to test in 7-mark sub-parts. Memorize the assumption list first.

**Assumptions (state explicitly for full marks):**
1. Working fluid is air (ideal gas, constant $c_p$, $\gamma$).
2. All processes are internally reversible; compression and expansion are isentropic.
3. Combustion is replaced by constant-pressure heat addition $q_{in}$.
4. Exhaust is constant-pressure heat rejection $q_{out}$.
5. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.

**Step 1 — Express each state using isentropic relations.**

For isentropic process 1→2 (compression):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

For isentropic process 3→4 (expansion), the pressure ratio across the turbine equals the compressor pressure ratio:

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the energy equation for each open-system component.**

Compressor work input (per unit mass):

$$w_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

Turbine work output (per unit mass):

$$w_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$$

Net specific work:

$$w_{net} = w_T - w_C = c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Express the heat input from combustion.**

Heat added at constant pressure per unit mass:

$$q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Form the thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{th,Brayton} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]}{c_p (T_3 - T_2)}$$

Substituting $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and simplifying:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}$$

**Numerical sanity check (Hydrogen-fired turbine, modern F-class):**
Take $r_p = 18$, $\gamma = 1.4$ for hot gas:
$(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$
$r_p^{0.2857} = 18^{0.2857} \approx 2.276$
$\eta_{th} \approx 1 - 1/2.276 \approx 56\%$

This matches commercial F-class combined-cycle efficiencies reported in the 60–62% range after waste-heat recovery.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Work (per unit mass)

The optimum pressure ratio maximizes $w_{net}$ for a given $T_3$ and $T_1$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $T_2$ and $T_4$ in terms of $r_p$.**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}, \quad T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Net work expression:**

$$w_{net} = c_p \left[ \left( T_3 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} \right) - \left( T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - T_1 \right) \right]$$

$$w_{net} = c_p \left[ T_3 \left( 1 - r_p^{-a} \right) - T_1 \left( r_p^{a} - 1 \right) \right], \quad \text{where } a = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $r_p$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dw_{net}}{dr_p} = c_p \left[ T_3 \cdot a \cdot r_p^{-a-1} - T_1 \cdot a \cdot r_p^{a-1} \right] = 0$$

$$T_3 \cdot r_p^{-a-1} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{a-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $r_p$:**

$$r_p^{2a} = \frac{T_3}{T_1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r_p^{2(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_3}{T_1}$$

$$\boxed{r_{p,opt} = \left( \frac{T_3}{T_1} \right)^{\gamma / 2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Numerical check:** For $T_3 = 1800\ \text{K}$, $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$:
$T_3/T_1 = 6$
Exponent $= 1.4 / (2 \times 0.4) = 1.75$
$r_{p,opt} = 6^{1.75} \approx 23.0$

This matches the operating pressure ratio of modern GE 9HA and Siemens SGT-9000HL turbines (~23–24:1).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Air Demand for Hydrogen Combustion

Given: Combustion reaction $\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O}$
Molecular masses: $H_2 = 2.016$ kg/kmol; $O_2 = 32$ kg/kmol; $N_2 = 28.014$ kg/kmol.
Air composition (mass): 23.2% O₂, 76.8% N₂.

**Step 1 — Oxygen demand per kg of H₂:**

For 4 kg H₂, we need 32 kg O₂. So:
$\text{O}_2$ per kg H₂ = $32/4 = 8$ kg O₂ / kg H₂

**Step 2 — Air demand (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass):**

$(A/F)_{st} = \frac{8}{0.232} = 34.48\ \text{kg air/kg H}_2$

Rounded to **34.33** in many references; use 34.3 for KTU.

**Step 3 — Combustion products:**

For 1 kg H₂:
- O₂ consumed: 8 kg → produces 9 kg H₂O (mass balance: 1+8=9)
- N₂ from air: $34.3 \times 0.768 = 26.34$ kg
- Total products: $9 + 26.34 = 35.34$ kg flue gas per kg H₂
- H₂O mass fraction in flue gas: $9/35.34 = 25.5\%$ — very high compared to ~18% for methane combustion.

This high water content is precisely why **hot-end blade cooling must use steam (not air)** to avoid condensation-related corrosion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Fuels in Gas Turbines

| Property | Natural Gas (CH₄) | Hydrogen (H₂) | Implication for Turbine |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 50.0 | **120.0** | H₂ carries 2.4× energy per kg |
| Density (kg/m³, STP) | 0.72 | **0.0899** | H₂ needs ~8× larger storage volume |
| Stoich. A/F (mass) | 17.2 | **34.3** | H₂ needs 2× more combustion air |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 0.4 | **2.5–3.5** | H₂ flashbacks easily |
| Flammability (vol%) | 5–15 | **4–75** | H₂ ignites over much wider range |
| Flame temp (K) | ~2223 | ~2823 | H₂ produces 600 K higher $T_{ad}$ |
| CO₂ emissions | 56 kg/GJ | **0** | Zero-carbon operation |
| NOx tendency | Moderate | High (thermal) | Requires lean burn & SCR |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic: Hydrogen Gas Turbine (Open Brayton Cycle) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **process flow and functional blocks** of a hydrogen-fired gas turbine system, including fuel delivery, combustion, expansion, and exhaust treatment. This is the KTU-recommended visualization approach for showing complex physical systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Air Inlet] --> B1[Inlet Air Filter and Silencer]
    B1 --> B2[Axial Flow Compressor Stage 1 to 18]
    B2 --> B3[High Pressure Compressed Air Manifold]
    B3 --> C1[Hydrogen Fuel Supply System]
    B3 --> C2[Combustor Liner with LPM or RQL Burners]
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3[High Temperature Combustion Zone 1600 to 1800 C]
    C3 --> D1[First Stage Nozzle Guide Vanes]
    D1 --> D2[High Pressure Turbine Section 1]
    D2 --> D3[Intermediate Pressure Turbine Section 2]
    D3 --> D4[Low Pressure Turbine Section 3]
    D4 --> E1[Exhaust Diffuser and Stack]
    E1 --> E2[SCR NOx Reduction Catalyst Unit]
    E2 --> E3[Heat Recovery Steam Generator for CCGT]
    E3 --> E4[Steam Turbine for Combined Cycle]
    E4 --> F1[Synchronous Electrical Generator]
    D2 --> F1
    D3 --> F1
    D4 --> F1
    B2 -.driveshaft.-> D2
    G1[Hydrogen Storage Tank Pressurized or Lined Cavern] --> C1
    G2[Pressure Regulation and Preheating Skid] --> C1
    H1[Control and Safety PLC System] --> C2
    H1 --> E2
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU exam tip — be ready to label the blocks):**
- **A → B2** is the *air side*: ambient air is filtered, then compressed to ~18:1 ratio.
- **C1 → C2** is the *fuel side*: hydrogen from storage is pressure-regulated and injected into the combustor.
- **C3 → D4** is the *expansion side*: hot combustion gases at $T_3 \approx 1700\ \text{°C}$ expand through three turbine sections, dropping to ~600 °C at exhaust.
- **E1 → E3** is the *post-treatment side*: NOₓ reduction + heat recovery for combined-cycle operation.
- **D2, D3, D4 → F1** shows the mechanical coupling: all turbine sections share a shaft driving the generator.

### 4.2 Schematic: Hydrogen Combustion Technology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Conventional Diffusion Flame] -->|High NOx, Stable| Q1[Not used for H2]
    P2[Lean Premixed LPM] -->|Low NOx, Flashback Risk| Q2[Modern H2 systems]
    P3[Rich Quench Lean RQL] -->|Low NOx, Robust| Q3[GE H-class standard]
    P4[Catalytic Combustion] -->|Ultra Low NOx, Durability Issue| Q4[Experimental]
    P5[Micro Mix Combustion] -->|Uniform Mixing, Compact| Q5[Next Gen]
    P6[Sequential Combustion] -->|Highest Efficiency| Q6[Siemens HL turbines]
```

### 4.3 Process Diagram: H₂-Specific Safety Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Hydrogen Storage Tank] --> S2[Pressure Transmitter]
    S2 --> S3[Safety Interlock PLC]
    S3 --> S4[Automatic Shutoff Valve ESD]
    S4 --> S5[Combustor Inlet Manifold]
    S5 --> S6[Continuous H2 Leak Detector Array]
    S6 --> S3
    S3 --> S7[Emergency Nitrogen Purge System]
    S3 --> S8[Venting and Flare Stack]
    S9[HVAC with ATEX Rated Explosion Proof Enclosure] -.ventilation.-> S5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU practical relevance:** When answering 14-mark Part B questions on hydrogen safety in turbines, **always mention** the inert nitrogen purge — it is the single most cited safety feature examiners look for.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a hydrogen turbine. Mention any two key advantages it has over a natural gas turbine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — write in 3 distinct points):**

A hydrogen turbine is a rotating machine that converts the chemical energy of hydrogen combustion into mechanical shaft work via a Brayton cycle, and typically drives an electrical generator.

**Advantages over natural gas turbines:**
1. **Zero CO₂ emissions** during operation, since the only combustion product is water vapor (H₂O). This aligns with global decarbonization targets.
2. **Higher mass-energy density** of hydrogen (LHV = 120 MJ/kg vs 50 MJ/kg for CH₄), enabling higher specific energy output per kilogram of fuel.
3. (Optional 3rd point) Faster combustion kinetics allow higher power density per unit combustor volume.

*(Examiner note: Award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for the two distinct advantages. One mark is reserved for clarity and correct terminology.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List four major challenges encountered when using pure hydrogen as a fuel in gas turbines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — enumerate four points with one-line explanation each):**

1. **Flashback** — Hydrogen's high flame speed (~3 m/s) allows the flame to travel upstream into the premix region, damaging hardware.
2. **Auto-ignition risk** — Broad flammability limits (4–75 vol%) and very low minimum ignition energy (0.017 mJ) cause unintended ignition.
3. **High NOx formation** — Adiabatic flame temperature > 2800 K promotes thermal NOx via the Zeldovich mechanism.
4. **Hydrogen embrittlement** — Atomic hydrogen diffuses into metal alloys (especially high-strength steels), causing cracking and reduced fatigue life.

*(Award 0.75 marks per correctly stated challenge. Brief justification expected.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Module 4 — Internal Choice Set A)]**

**Q3. (a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a hydrogen-fueled gas turbine operating on the open Brayton cycle. Discuss the role of the combustor, turbine, and compressor, and list the modifications needed for safe hydrogen operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen turbine operates with inlet conditions $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$, $P_1 = 100\ \text{kPa}$, compressor pressure ratio $r_p = 16$, and turbine inlet temperature $T_3 = 1600\ \text{K}$. Assume air-standard Brayton cycle with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Calculate:
   (i) the compressor exit temperature $T_2$,
   (ii) the turbine exit temperature $T_4$,
   (iii) the net specific work output, and
   (iv) the thermal efficiency of the cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks — distribute as follows):**
- Definition + cycle diagram: 2 marks
- Component-wise role: 3 marks
- Hydrogen-specific modifications: 2 marks

A hydrogen gas turbine operates on the **open Brayton cycle**. Atmospheric air is drawn in through the inlet, compressed in the **axial/centrifugal compressor** (typically 1→18 bar in modern F-class units), then mixed with hydrogen in the **combustor**, where exothermic reaction $\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O}$ raises the gas temperature to ~1700 K. The high-energy gas expands through **multistage turbine blades**, producing shaft work used to drive the compressor and the generator. The exhaust gas (mostly water vapor + N₂) is released to the atmosphere or fed to a HRSG for combined-cycle operation.

**Modifications for hydrogen:**
- Use **lean premixed (LPM)** or **rich-quench-lean (RQL)** combustors to suppress thermal NOx.
- Install **flashback arrestors** and **hydrogen-compatible fuel nozzles**.
- Provide **nitrogen purge system** for safe shutdown.
- Use **austenitic stainless steels** (e.g., Inconel 718) for hot-end components to resist hydrogen embrittlement.
- Implement **continuous H₂ leak detectors** and ATEX-rated enclosures.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1.75 marks each sub-part):**

**Given:** $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$, $r_p = 16$, $T_3 = 1600\ \text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$.

Compute exponent $a = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$.
$r_p^{a} = 16^{0.2857}$.

$\ln(16) = 2.7726$, so $16^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.7726} = e^{0.7922} \approx 2.208$

**(i) Compressor exit temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{a} = 300 \times 2.208 = 662.4\ \text{K}$$

**(ii) Turbine exit temperature:**

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{a}} = \frac{1600}{2.208} = 724.6\ \text{K}$$

**(iii) Net specific work:**

$$w_{net} = c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$$

$$w_{net} = 1.005 \times \left[ (1600 - 724.6) - (662.4 - 300) \right]$$

$$w_{net} = 1.005 \times \left[ 875.4 - 362.4 \right] = 1.005 \times 513.0 = 515.6\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{w_{net}}{c_p (T_3 - T_2)} = \frac{515.6}{1.005 \times (1600 - 662.4)} = \frac{515.6}{942.7} = 0.547$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 54.7\%}$$

*(This is the ideal Brayton efficiency; actual values are 5–10% lower due to compressor/turbine isentropic efficiencies < 1.)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Module 4 — Internal Choice Set B)]**

**Q4. (a)** Explain the four main hydrogen combustion technologies used in modern gas turbines (Lean Premixed, Rich-Quench-Lean, Catalytic, Micro-Mix). Compare them on the basis of NOx emissions and flashback resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pure hydrogen-fired gas turbine receives fuel at a rate of $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 5\ \text{kg/s}$. If the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio is 34.3 kg air/kg H₂ and the equivalence ratio used is $\phi = 0.5$ (i.e., ultra-lean operation), calculate:
   (i) the actual mass of air required per second,
   (ii) the mass flow rate of exhaust gases, and
   (iii) the total heat input rate, taking LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Combustion Technologies (7 marks — 1.75 marks each technology):**

- **Lean Premixed (LPM):** Fuel and air are premixed before entering the combustor at equivalence ratio $\phi < 0.7$. *Lowest NOx* (peak $T < 1850\ \text{K}$). **High flashback risk** because flame can travel into the premixer.
- **Rich-Quench-Lean (RQL):** Primary zone operates fuel-rich (φ > 1), where lack of O₂ suppresses thermal NOx; secondary air dilutes rapidly to lean conditions. **Moderate-to-low NOx, very high flashback resistance** — preferred for utility-scale H₂ turbines.
- **Catalytic:** Combustion initiated by a catalyst surface at 700–900 K, well below auto-ignition temperature. **Ultra-low NOx**, but catalyst deactivation over time and limited power density.
- **Micro-Mix:** Hundreds of micrometer-scale fuel/air jets produce thousands of small flames with uniform mixing. **Low NOx, moderate flashback resistance**, excellent turndown ratio.

*(Comparison table expected for full marks. Examiners often award a 1-mark bonus for clear tabulation.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 5\ \text{kg/s}$, $(A/F)_{st} = 34.3$, $\phi = 0.5$, $LHV = 120\ \text{MJ/kg}$.

**Recall:** Equivalence ratio $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{st}} \;\Rightarrow\; (A/F)_{actual} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{st}}{\phi}$

$$(A/F)_{actual} = \frac{34.3}{0.5} = 68.6\ \text{kg air/kg H}_2$$

**(i) Actual mass of air per second:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times (A/F)_{actual} = 5 \times 68.6 = 343\ \text{kg/s}$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate of exhaust gases:**

Combustion of 1 kg H₂ produces 9 kg H₂O (Section 3.3 derivation).
N₂ entering with air = $68.6 \times 0.768 = 52.7$ kg N₂ per kg H₂.
Excess O₂ leaving (since $\phi = 0.5$, only half the air's O₂ is consumed):
O₂ supplied per kg H₂ = $68.6 \times 0.232 = 15.92$ kg
O₂ consumed = 8 kg (stoichiometric)
O₂ remaining = $15.92 - 8 = 7.92$ kg

Total flue gas per kg H₂ = $9 + 52.7 + 7.92 = 69.62$ kg

$$\dot{m}_{exhaust} = 5 \times 69.62 = 348.1\ \text{kg/s}$$

**Quick alternative check:** Mass conservation: $\dot{m}_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_{H_2} = 343 + 5 = 348\ \text{kg/s}$ ✓ *(matches within rounding)*

**(iii) Total heat input rate:**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV = 5 \times 120 = 600\ \text{MW}$$

*(Award 0.25 mark for correct unit conversion recognition: kg/s × MJ/kg = MW.)*

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} = 343\ \text{kg/s}, \quad \dot{m}_{exhaust} \approx 348\ \text{kg/s}, \quad \dot{Q}_{in} = 600\ \text{MW}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** use the higher heating value (HHV = 141.88 MJ/kg) when the question states "LHV." Examiner deducts 1 mark silently.
> - **Always** state assumptions (ideal gas, air-standard, constant $c_p$) before starting derivations. Marks are explicitly reserved for assumption lists.
> - **Do not** confuse "mass flow of exhaust" with "mass flow of air entering combustor." Show the balance: $\dot{m}_{ex} = \dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_{fuel}$ as a cross-check — it is a free 0.5 mark.
> - **Avoid** using `|` in answer sheets when writing absolute values; use `\vert` (LaTeX) or simply write *mod x*.
> - **In block diagrams**, always label all streams (air, fuel, exhaust, water) and show energy conversion steps — vague unlabeled boxes lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

✅ **Hydrogen Turbine = Brayton cycle machine burning H₂** for zero-carbon power generation.
✅ **LHV of H₂ = 119.96 MJ/kg** (memorize this — examiners test it).
✅ **Stoichiometric A/F ratio = 34.3 kg air/kg H₂** (vs 17.2 for methane — exactly double).
✅ **Ideal Brayton efficiency formula:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$
✅ **Optimum pressure ratio:** $r_{p,opt} = (T_3/T_1)^{\gamma/2(\gamma-1)}$
✅ **Net specific work:** $w_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$
✅ **Four hydrogen-specific challenges:** Flashback, Auto-ignition, NOx, Hydrogen Embrittlement.
✅ **Four combustion technologies:** LPM, RQL, Catalytic, Micro-Mix (compare on NOx + flashback).
✅ **Modern H-class TIT:** $T_3 \approx 1600\text{–}1700\ \text{°C}$; pressure ratio $\approx 18\text{–}24$.
✅ **Safety must-mentions:** Nitrogen purge, ATEX enclosures, leak detectors, ESD valves.
✅ **Comparative numerical anchors:**
   - H₂ flame speed ≈ 3 m/s (≈ 8× methane).
   - H₂ flammability = 4–75% vol (vs 5–15% for CH₄).
   - H₂ min ignition energy = 0.017 mJ (≈ 10× lower than CH₄).
   - H₂O content in exhaust = ~25% (vs ~18% for CH₄).
✅ **One-liner for any definition question:** *"A hydrogen turbine is a Brayton-cycle rotating machine that converts the chemical energy of hydrogen combustion (zero-CO₂, water-only exhaust) into mechanical and electrical power."*

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 Hydrogen Gas Burner — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen Gas Burner** is an engineered combustion device designed to safely mix hydrogen fuel with an oxidizer (typically air or pure oxygen) in a precisely controlled ratio, deliver the mixture to a combustion zone, ignite it, and stabilize the resulting flame for the purpose of releasing thermal energy in a usable form. The device integrates a **fuel delivery manifold**, an **oxidizer inlet system**, a **mixing chamber** (or a diffusion zone), a **flame stabilization mechanism** (burner port, porous matrix, or catalytic surface), and critical **safety subsystems** such as flashback arrestors, flame arrestors, and flame sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OECHT616 – Module 4):**
> A hydrogen gas burner is a hardware component in which hydrogen is combusted with air/oxygen to liberate its chemical energy in a controlled manner. Unlike hydrocarbon burners, hydrogen burners must address unique challenges such as **high flame speed**, **wide flammability limits**, **low minimum ignition energy**, and **invisible flame radiation**, making burner geometry and safety design critical.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Furnace"

Imagine a **gas stove in your kitchen** that uses LPG. You turn the knob, gas flows out, mixes with air, and a blue flame appears. Now, replace that LPG with **pure hydrogen**. The physical concept is identical — *fuel + oxidizer + ignition = heat*. However, hydrogen behaves like a **whispering furnace**:

- 🔊 **LPG flame**: A loud, visible, easy-to-see campfire
- 🔇 **Hydrogen flame**: A nearly **invisible, fast-moving whisper** that can sneak back through tiny gaps

The challenge for the engineer is to design a burner that **respects hydrogen's silent, fast, and highly diffusive nature** — which is why hydrogen burners look different from conventional ones (smaller ports, longer flame arrestors, water-cooled jackets).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants of Hydrogen Combustion (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**: **120 MJ/kg** (≈ 3× gasoline)
> - **Flammability Limits in Air**: **4 % – 75 %** (by volume)
> - **Flammability Limits in Oxygen**: **4 % – 94 %** (by volume)
> - **Auto-ignition Temperature**: **585 °C**
> - **Minimum Ignition Energy**: **0.017 mJ** (extremely low!)
> - **Laminar Burning Velocity in Air**: **2.5 – 3.5 m/s**
> - **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Air)**: **~2400 K** (≈ 2045 °C)
> - **Quenching Distance (Air)**: **~0.64 mm**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass)**: **34.3 : 1**

## 1.3 Why Hydrogen Burners Differ from Hydrocarbon Burners

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methane (CH₄) | Propane (C₃H₈) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flame Visibility | **Nearly Invisible** | Light Blue | Bright Yellow/Blue |
| Flame Speed (m/s) | **2.5 – 3.5** | 0.4 | 0.46 |
| Ignition Energy | **0.017 mJ** | 0.29 mJ | 0.26 mJ |
| Flammability Range (% vol) | **4 – 75** | 5 – 15 | 2.1 – 9.5 |
| Quenching Gap (mm) | **0.64** | 2.0 | 1.8 |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **120** | 50 | 46 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The combination of *low ignition energy* + *high flame speed* + *wide flammability limits* is what makes hydrogen **the most dangerous common fuel to handle** in burner systems. Every design choice flows from this triad.

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Stage by Stage

A hydrogen gas burner operates through **five sequential stages**. Each stage must be precisely engineered; failure of any single stage can lead to flashback, blow-off, or explosion.

1. **Fuel Supply & Pressure Regulation**
   - Hydrogen enters at regulated pressure (typically **1 – 4 bar** for industrial burners).
   - A **pressure regulator** + **solenoid valve** + **flashback arrestor** form the safety chain.
   - Flow is metered via **orifice plates** or **mass flow controllers**.

2. **Air/Oxidizer Supply**
   - Air is delivered by a **blower or compressor** (forced-draft) or by **natural draft** (atmospheric burners).
   - The oxidizer stream may be **preheated** in recuperative burners to improve efficiency.

3. **Mixing**
   - **Premixed burners**: Fuel and air mix *upstream* of the burner port (Bunsen-type).
   - **Diffusion (non-premixed) burners**: Fuel and air mix *at* the flame front.
   - Premixed designs give higher efficiency and lower emissions, but are more prone to **flashback**.

4. **Ignition**
   - Ignition is achieved by a **high-energy spark**, **hot surface**, or **pilot flame**.
   - The required ignition energy is only **0.017 mJ**, so even static discharge can ignite the mixture — hence **grounding and antistatic measures** are mandatory.

5. **Flame Stabilization & Heat Transfer**
   - The flame is anchored at the burner port by the balance of **jet velocity** vs **flame speed**.
   - Heat is transferred to the load by **convection**, **conduction**, and (in oxygen-rich flames) **radiation** from water vapor.

## 2.2 Classification of Hydrogen Burners

Hydrogen burners are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By Mixing Method
- **Premixed Burners** (e.g., Bunsen, surface combustion)
- **Diffusion Flame Burners** (e.g., welding torches)
- **Partially Premixed Burners**

### Axis B — By Flame Geometry
- **Jet / Impinging Flame**
- **Flat Flame** (e.g., porous plug, McKenna burner)
- **Swirl Flame** (high-intensity, industrial)
- **Radiant Tube Burners**

### Axis C — By Application / Power Density
- **Low-power** (lab burners, ~kW range)
- **Medium-power** (industrial heating, ~MW range)
- **High-power** (boilers, steel processing, ~tens of MW)

> [!NOTE]
> **Popular Special-Purpose Designs (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Porous Media Burner (PMB)**: Combustion occurs *inside* a porous ceramic matrix. This dramatically widens the operating range and **suppresses flashback** because the pores are smaller than the quenching distance.
> - **Catalytic Burner**: Uses a platinum/palladium catalyst to oxidize H₂ at low temperatures (200–400 °C) — used in **fuel cell heaters and domestic appliances**.
> - **Oxy-Hydrogen Burner**: Burns pure H₂ + O₂ for ultra-high temperatures (e.g., quartz glass processing, ~3100 °C).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you will need for KTU ESE problems on hydrogen burners. **Memorize the units and the limiting conditions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric combustion of H₂ | $\mathrm{2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O}$ | — | Pure, no carbon, no soot |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $\mathrm{AFR_{stoich} = 34.3}$ | kg air / kg H₂ | Derived from molar masses |
| 3 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (molar) | $\mathrm{AFR_{stoich,mol} = 2.38}$ | mol air / mol H₂ | (4N₂+O₂)/2 ≈ 4.76/2 |
| 4 | Equivalence Ratio | $\mathrm{\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}}$ | — | φ < 1: lean; φ > 1: rich |
| 5 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $\mathrm{T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{Y_{F} \cdot LHV}{c_{p,products}}}$ | K | Stoichiometric air: ~2400 K |
| 6 | Laminar Burning Velocity (H₂-air) | $\mathrm{S_L \approx 2.5 - 3.5}$ | m/s | Function of φ, T, P |
| 7 | Flashback Condition | $\mathrm{V_{jet} < S_L}$ | m/s | Flame propagates upstream |
| 8 | Blow-off Condition | $\mathrm{V_{jet} \gg S_L}$ | m/s | Flame detaches from port |
| 9 | Heat Release Rate | $\mathrm{\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}$ | kW | Industrial sizing formula |
| 10 | Quenching Distance (H₂-air) | $\mathrm{d_q \approx 0.64\ \text{mm}}$ | mm | Port diameter must be **larger** than d_q |
| 11 | Peclet Number (Porous Burner) | $\mathrm{Pe = \dfrac{u \cdot L}{\alpha}}$ | — | Low Pe → effective heat recirculation |
| 12 | Sadiq-Bell-Glassman Limit (NOx) | $\mathrm{T_{ad} > 1800\ K \Rightarrow \text{thermal } NO_x \uparrow}$ | K | Drives need for low-NOx burners |

> [!WARNING]

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen burners are deployed across:
- 🏭 **Steel & metal heat treatment** (replacing natural gas to decarbonize industry)
- 🧪 **Semiconductor & quartz-glass processing** (requires ultra-clean, carbon-free flame)
- 🏠 **Domestic boilers & cookers** (Japan/EU pilot programs, e.g., ENE-FARM type units)
- 🔬 **Laboratories** (Bunsen-style premixed H₂ burners for analytical work)
- ✂️ **Oxy-hydrogen welding/cutting** (jewelry, plumbing)
- 🚀 **Rocket propulsion** (LOX/LH₂ engines — fundamentally a hydrogen-oxygen burner)

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for H₂

The balanced chemical equation for complete hydrogen combustion in air is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\mathrm{H_2} \; + \; \mathrm{O_2} \; + \; 3.76\,\mathrm{N_2} \; &\longrightarrow \; 2\mathrm{H_2O} \; + \; 3.76\,\mathrm{N_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Count moles of fuel and air per mole of H₂ burned.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per 2 mol H}_2 &: \quad 1\ \mathrm{mol\ O_2} + 3.76\ \mathrm{mol\ N_2} \\
\text{Per 1 mol H}_2 &: \quad 0.5\ \mathrm{mol\ O_2} + 1.88\ \mathrm{mol\ N_2} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Total air per mol H}_2 = 0.5 + 1.88 = 2.38\ \text{mol air}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass basis using molecular weights** $M_{H_2} = 2\ \mathrm{kg/kmol}$ and $M_{\mathrm{air}} = 28.97\ \mathrm{kg/kmol}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{\mathrm{mol}} &= \dfrac{2.38\ \mathrm{mol\ air}}{1\ \mathrm{mol\ H_2}} = 2.38 \\[4pt]
AFR_{\mathrm{mass}} &= \dfrac{2.38 \times 28.97}{1 \times 2} = \dfrac{68.95}{2} = 34.48
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** $AFR_{\mathrm{stoich}} \approx \mathbf{34.3\ \text{kg air / kg H}_2}$ and $AFR_{\mathrm{mol}} \approx \mathbf{2.38\ \text{mol air / mol H}_2}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Stoichiometric H₂–Air)

We apply an **energy balance on the adiabatic combustion** at constant pressure:

$$
\sum_i n_i u_i \big\vert_{T_0} = \sum_j n_j h_j \big\vert_{T_{ad}}
$$

For the stoichiometric reaction, products are **2 mol H₂O + 3.76 mol N₂**, reactants are **2 mol H₂ + 1 mol O₂ + 3.76 mol N₂**. Using mean $c_p$ values for the products and a reference of $T_0 = 298\ \mathrm{K}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy released} &= 2 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f(\mathrm{H_2O,g}) = 2 \times (-241.82\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}) \\
&= -483.64\ \mathrm{kJ\ per\ 2\ mol\ H_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy balance (per 2 mol H₂, adiabatic, no work):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\mathrm{H_2O}}\, c_{p,\mathrm{H_2O}}\, (T_{ad} - T_0) + n_{\mathrm{N_2}}\, c_{p,\mathrm{N_2}}\, (T_{ad} - T_0) = 483.64\ \mathrm{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $c_{p,\mathrm{H_2O}} \approx 41\ \mathrm{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, $c_{p,\mathrm{N_2}} \approx 30\ \mathrm{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, and $n_{\mathrm{N_2}} = 3.76$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bigl(2 \times 41 + 3.76 \times 30\bigr) (T_{ad} - 298) &= 483\,640\ \mathrm{J} \\[4pt]
\bigl(82 + 112.8\bigr)(T_{ad} - 298) &= 483\,640 \\[4pt]
194.8\,(T_{ad} - 298) &= 483\,640 \\[4pt]
T_{ad} - 298 &= 2482.5\ \mathrm{K} \\[4pt]
T_{ad} &\approx 2780\ \mathrm{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result (theoretical, no dissociation):** $T_{ad} \approx 2780\ \mathrm{K}$.  
> With **dissociation** of H₂O at high T, the **realistic adiabatic flame temperature** for stoichiometric H₂–air is $T_{ad} \approx \mathbf{2400\ K}$ (≈ **2045 °C**). The KTU board accepts both values depending on the problem's stated assumptions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flashback and Blow-off Limits

The **flame anchoring condition** at a burner port is governed by the balance between the **jet velocity** of the unburned mixture ($V_j$) and the **fundamental laminar burning velocity** ($S_L$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stable flame:} \quad & V_j \;\geq\; S_L \quad \text{(and } V_j \;\leq\; S_L \cdot K_{ext}\text{)} \\[4pt]
\text{Flashback:} \quad & V_j \;<\; S_L \quad \text{or} \quad d_{\mathrm{port}} \;<\; d_q \\[4pt]
\text{Blow-off:} \quad & V_j \;\gg\; S_L \quad \text{(mixture exits faster than flame can propagate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quantitative example (typical KTU problem):** A hydrogen–air premixed burner has $d_{\mathrm{port}} = 0.5\ \mathrm{mm}$. Will flashback occur? Use $d_q \approx 0.64\ \mathrm{mm}$ for H₂–air.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\mathrm{port}} &= 0.5\ \mathrm{mm} \\[2pt]
d_q &= 0.64\ \mathrm{mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Since } d_{\mathrm{port}} < d_q &\Rightarrow \text{Flashback WILL occur.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design fix:** Increase the port diameter to **≥ 0.7 mm** *or* use a **porous plug** to block flame propagation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Grabber:** A common 3-mark question asks *“Why is a hydrogen burner prone to flashback?”* — answer: (1) high $S_L$, (2) very small $d_q$ (0.64 mm), (3) low ignition energy, (4) wide flammability range. Cite all four for full marks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Release & Burner Sizing

Given a desired thermal output $\dot{Q}$ (kW), the **hydrogen mass flow rate** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{LHV} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}\ [\mathrm{kW}]}{120\,000\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{120}\ \mathrm{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** A laboratory hydrogen burner must deliver $\dot{Q} = 60\ \mathrm{kW}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} &= \dfrac{60}{120} = 0.5\ \mathrm{g/s} = 1.8\ \mathrm{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{\mathrm{air,\,stoich}} &= 0.0005 \times 34.3 = 0.01715\ \mathrm{kg/s} = 61.7\ \mathrm{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Conclusion:** Even a 60 kW burner consumes only **0.5 g/s of hydrogen** — a vivid demonstration of H₂'s exceptional energy density by mass.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Burner Type | Mixing | Flashback Risk | Typical Power | NOx Level | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Premixed (Bunsen) | Upstream | **High** | W – kW | Low | Lab burners |
| Diffusion Torch | At flame | **Low** | kW – MW | Medium | Welding |
| Porous Media | Inside matrix | **Very Low** | kW – MW | **Very Low** | Industrial radiant |
| Catalytic | Surface | **Negligible** | W – kW | Negligible | Domestic heater |
| Swirl-Stabilized | Premixed, swirling | Medium | MW | Medium | Boiler |
| Oxy-Hydrogen | Premixed O₂ | High | kW – MW | Low | Glass, quartz |
